title: Marathon pacing choices and race day clarity
excerpt: JP Flavin and Priscah Cherono won the 2025 Marathon Project, showcasing strong performances. Detailed splits and prize information are available for analysis.
body:
Marathon pacing is not about hitting a fixed target from the gun, but about making smart real-time decisions that match effort to conditions, as JP Flavin and Priscah Cherono demonstrated at the 2025 Marathon Project.

At the 2025 Marathon Project, JP Flavin and Priscah Cherono executed pacing strategies that revealed careful balancing of effort and endurance. Their splits highlight how precise timing of surges and conservation of energy played a role in their victories on a course designed for fast times. This offers more than winning results; it provides an opportunity to examine the nuanced decisions that define an effective race day plan.

For runners of all levels, understanding how to distribute effort over long distances is essential. The winners' approach shows that pacing is not merely about speed but about adapting to conditions and responding to the race flow. Observing such performances encourages reflection on personal pacing judgment and the value of measured progression rather than all-out exertion from the start.

Study your own race splits after every event. Consistent second-half pacing, even slightly slower than the first half, typically produces better outcomes than going out hard and fading. Build that habit in training first.
keyTakeaway: Consider pacing as a strategic dialogue with your body and the race rather than a fixed target to hit from the outset.
